source: Nigeria National Programme on Immunisation (NPI) — Federal Ministry of Health; WHO/UNICEF 2024 country profile; Paediatric Association of Nigeria (PAN) Recommended Schedule 2020 (reviewed periodically)
source_notes: Public NPI schedule (free at government facilities) composed from UNICEF Nigeria + WHO 2024 country profile + PMC4139536 (Current Trends of Immunization in Nigeria). PAN recommended additions (private uptake) marked with category='recommended'. IPV: Nigeria introduced IPV1 at 14 weeks into routine schedule; IPV2 not yet introduced as of 2024 per WHO country profile.
verification_notes: Do not treat this seed as fully ready until row-level review against the current WHO/XMart 2024 JRF schedule is complete. Current WHO/XMart rows for NGA include schedule items that require reconciliation with this seed before clinical use, including IPV timing, PCV product label, HPV4 at age 9 years, malaria vaccine subnational introduction, MenA, yellow fever, and Vitamin A as a supplement rather than a vaccine.
source_year_or_release_date: 2024 WHO/UNICEF JRF schedule data; seed last reviewed 2026-04-22
scope: Routine childhood immunisation, birth to 72 months (6 years). Compulsory (NPI) and PAN-recommended antigens with category distinction.
context_note: In cross-border reconciliation into Egypt, NPI-compulsory doses are the reliable signal on a Nigerian card. Yellow Fever and Meningococcal A doses are NPI-routine in Nigeria but are not on the Egyptian schedule — these remain as Nigerian-only antigens and should be preserved on the record but not counted as 'missing' for Egypt.
key_differences_vs_egypt: Nigeria uses 6/10/14-week WHO-aligned primary series; Egypt uses 2/4/6-month series

Both Nigeria and Egypt use Pentavalent (DTPw + HepB + Hib) in public EPI; component-equivalence on those visits is straightforward. Egypt private practice may substitute Hexavalent (Hexyon / Infanrix Hexa) which adds IPV in the same shot.

Polio: Nigeria gives OPV 0/1/2/3 + a single IPV at 14 weeks. Egypt gives OPV at birth/2/4/6/9/12/18 months + a single IPV at 4 months only. The IPV count matches (one each) but the visit alignment differs.

HepB birth dose: Egypt mandates a discrete day-1 HepB dose (كبدي ب رضع); Nigeria's NPI does not include a routine HepB birth dose, so Nigerian cards typically do not satisfy Egypt's birth-dose requirement even when the full Pentavalent series is documented.
Nigeria gives Measles monovalent at 9 months; Egypt gives MMR at 12 months
Nigeria has routine Rotavirus (Rota1 6w, Rota2 10w) in NPI; Egypt does not
Nigeria has routine PCV13 (6/10/14w) in NPI; Egypt PCV is recommended/private only
Nigeria has routine Yellow Fever at 9 months; Egypt does not (YF not endemic)
Nigeria has Meningococcal A (MenAfriVac) at 9 months in meningitis-belt states; Egypt does not

doses.notes: At birth or as soon as possible. PAN guidance: skip birth dose if presenting after 2 weeks without documentation is typically still given in Nigeria; verify by local practice.
Birth dose (HBV0). Standalone monovalent HepB at birth.
OPV0 at birth. Nigeria uses bivalent OPV (bOPV) in routine schedule.
OPV1 at 6 weeks. Co-administered with Pentavalent, PCV, Rotavirus.
Penta1 at 6 weeks. 5-in-1 covering DTPw (whole-cell pertussis), Hib, HepB. Does NOT include IPV — IPV is given separately at 14 weeks in Nigeria.
PCV13-1 at 6 weeks. Nigeria uses PCV13 in NPI since introduction.
doses.verification_source: UNICEF Nigeria Immunization Schedule; PAN 2020; PMC4139536

doses.notes: Rota1 at 6 weeks. Do not initiate after 15 weeks of age (PAN/WHO window). Nigeria introduced rotavirus into routine NPI nationally.
OPV2 at 10 weeks.
Rota2 at 10 weeks. Do not administer after 32 weeks of age.
OPV3 at 14 weeks.
Penta2 at 10 weeks.
Penta3 at 14 weeks. Completes primary DTP/Hib/HepB series.
PCV13-2 at 10 weeks.
PCV13-3 at 14 weeks. Completes primary PCV series.
IPV1 at 14 weeks. Introduced into NPI alongside OPV as part of global polio endgame. IPV2 not yet introduced into Nigerian NPI as of 2024 (per WHO country profile).
Measles monovalent at 9 months. Nigeria NPI gives Measles (not MMR) in routine programme.
Yellow Fever at 9 months. Single lifetime dose per current WHO guidance. Nigeria is yellow-fever-endemic; this is routine NPI.
Meningococcal A conjugate (MenAfriVac) at 9 months in meningitis-belt states (northern Nigeria). Not routine in all states; verify against child's state of residence.
Second measles dose at 15 months (MCV2) — phased into Nigerian NPI following WHO recommendation for 2-dose measles strategy.
DTaP booster at 12-15 months. PAN-recommended; availability varies — NPI booster uptake lower than primary series.
OPV booster at 12-15 months (PAN-recommended).
HPV introduced into Nigerian NPI for girls aged 9-14 in 2023. Single-dose schedule per WHO SAGE 2022 recommendation.

not_in_schedule.description: Antigens NOT in Nigeria's NPI that may be expected on schedules from other countries

antigens.notes: Nigeria uses Measles monovalent, not MMR. Mumps and Rubella components are not in public NPI.
Not in NPI. Private only (PAN recommends at 18 months).
Second IPV dose not yet introduced into Nigerian NPI (as of 2024).

interval_rules.notes: Dose 2 must be given before 32 weeks of age regardless of interval.

age_limits.notes: Per WHO: do not initiate rotavirus vaccination at or after 15 weeks of age due to intussusception risk.
Per WHO: do not administer any rotavirus dose at or after 32 weeks of age.
